coredata.dc:title: Modification of graphene oxide surfaces with 12-molybdophosphoric acid: Structural and antibacterial study
coredata.dc:creator: Jovanović, Svetlana
Holclajtner-Antunović, Ivanka
Uskoković-Marković, Snežana
Bajuk-Bogdanović, Danica
Pavlović, Vladimir
Tošić, Dragana
Milenković, Marina
Todorović Marković, Biljana
coredata.dc:description: Antibacterial properties of graphene oxide (GO) have been studied extensively in the last few years, while for polyoxometalates there are just a few researches. Herein, we prepared nanocomposites of GO and different amounts of 12-molybdophosphoric acid (MoPA) and analyzed their antibacterial activity and both structural and morphological properties. In nanocomposites with higher amounts of MoPA, graphene sheets were significantly changed with disrupted flat, graphene-like morphology of GO. In the nanocomposites with low MoPA content, flat GO morphology was preserved. Additionally, structural analyses showed some changes in symmetry of the Keggin anion as a consequence of interactions between GO and MoPA, the increase in structural disorder and the lowering of electron density in GO structure due to interaction with MoPA. We have studied antibacterial properties on the gram-positive: Staphylococcus aureus, Staphylococcus epidermidis, Bacillus subtilis; and the gram-negative bacteria: Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Salmonella abony. Only a mild antibacterial effect of GO and nanocomposites with low amount of MoPA against Bacillus subtilis was observed, while the rest of analyzed materials did not show any antibacterial activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
12-Molybdophosphoric acid
Antibacterial properties
Atomic force microscopy
Infrared spectroscopy
Raman spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide by modified Hummers method
Synthesis of graphene oxide – MoPA nanocomposites
Methods: AFM, SEM EDS, FTIR and Raman measurements

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministarstvo Prosvete, Nauke i Tehnološkog Razvoja

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Research was supported by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia , project no. 172003 and 172043 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM images and corresponding cross-section profiles (in the upper left corners) of GO (a), GO-MoPA-NW (b) and GO-MoPA-W (c). The representative regions indicated with blue squares are used for Rq analysis of GO (d), GO-MoPA-NW (e) and GO-MoPA-W (f). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
SEM images of GO (a, b), GO-MoPA-NW (c, d) and GO-MoPA-W (e, f).
TEM images of GO (a) MoPA (b), GO-MoPA-W (c, d) and GO-MoPA-NW (e, f).

UV-Vis spectra of GO (black line), GO-MoPA-NW (red lower line) and GO-MoPA-W (green middle line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
ATR-FTIR spectra of GO, GO-MoPA-W, GO-MoPA-NW and MoPA.
Raman spectra of GO (1), GO-MoPA-NW (2), GO-MoPA-W (3).
AFM images of Bacillus subtilis: control (a), bacteria treated with GO-MoPA-NW (b) and GO-MoPA-W (c) at concentration 250 μg mL−1.
The results of SEM-EDS analysis for GO and GO-MoPA nanohybrids.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Standard deviation shown in brackets, based on three independent measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Positions of D, G and 2D bands and values of ID/IG ratio.
Defined MIC for tested samples prepared without DMSO.

head.ce:title: Modification of graphene oxide surfaces with 12-molybdophosphoric acid: Structural and antibacterial study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences, University of Belgrade, P.O. Box 522, 11001, Belgrade, Serbia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Physical Chemistry, University of Belgrade, P.O. Box 47, Belgrade, 11158, Serbia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Agriculture, University of Belgrade, P.O. Box 14, Belgrade-Zemun, 11081, Serbia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences, University of Belgrade, P.O. Box 522, 11001 Belgrade, Serbia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antibacterial properties of graphene oxide (GO) have been studied extensively in the last few years, while for polyoxometalates there are just a few researches. Herein, we prepared nanocomposites of GO and different amounts of 12-molybdophosphoric acid (MoPA) and analyzed their antibacterial activity and both structural and morphological properties. In nanocomposites with higher amounts of MoPA, graphene sheets were significantly changed with disrupted flat, graphene-like morphology of GO. In the nanocomposites with low MoPA content, flat GO morphology was preserved. Additionally, structural analyses showed some changes in symmetry of the Keggin anion as a consequence of interactions between GO and MoPA, the increase in structural disorder and the lowering of electron density in GO structure due to interaction with MoPA. We have studied antibacterial properties on the gram-positive: Staphylococcus aureus, Staphylococcus epidermidis, Bacillus subtilis; and the gram-negative bacteria: Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Salmonella abony. Only a mild antibacterial effect of GO and nanocomposites with low amount of MoPA against Bacillus subtilis was observed, while the rest of analyzed materials did not show any antibacterial activity.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is single atomic layer of sp2 hybridized C atoms. Excellent electronic, optical, mechanical and thermal properties of graphene make this material suitable candidate for application in sensors, energy storage, electronics, etc. [1]. Considering graphene structure, it is highly hydrophobic material, insoluble in water.

Introducing carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl and epoxy functional groups in the structure of graphene, hydrophilic, water soluble material was prepared: graphene oxide (GO). As a result of these structural changes, GO is not conductive, which limits certain applications in electronics, but superior properties like mechanical and chemical stability, large surface area, tunable optical and electrical properties make this material a promising candidate for application in energy storage/conversion, environment protection, photocatalysis for water splitting, purification of water and air, and as electrode materials for supercapacitors and lithium batteries, etc. [2,3]. Considered as low cost material, GO could be widely used in both environmental and clinical applications [4]. Due to its flexibility, water solubility, amphiphilicity, surface functionalization capability, relatively small size and large surface area, GO shows interesting biological properties. It has been studied as a platform for target drug delivery [5], photothermal therapy [6], biosensing [7] and bioimaging [8]. Among broad biological applications of GO, antibacterial effects are particularly interesting in the last few years.

The antibacterial effects of GO and its reduced form (rGO) were increasingly studied in the last 5 years [9,10]. The study of antimicrobial activity of GO against two bacteria Pseudomonas syringae and Xanthomonas campestris pv. undulosa and two fungal pathogens (Fusarium graminearum and Fusarium oxysporum) showed that GO had a powerful effect: killed nearly 90% of the bacteria and repressed 80% macroconidia germination at 500 mg mL−1. A proposed mechanism suggested that GO intertwines the bacteria and fungal spores with a wide range of aggregated graphene oxide sheets and leads to local perturbation of the cell membrane, the lowering of the membrane potential and causing the leakage of electrolytes of fungal spores. It was suggested that GO was mechanically wrapped around bacteria inducing local damage to the cell membrane and to cell lysis [11]. Also nanocomposites based on GO and silver nanoparticles showed antifungal activity against phytopathogen Fusarium graminearum at a relatively low concentration of 4.68 μg mL−1 (minimum inhibition concentration (MIC)) [12]. Here, an antibacterial mechanism was assigned to the synergistic effect of GO-AgNPs: physical injury and chemically reactive oxygen species generation. Another nanocomposite based on GO and graphitic carbon nitride showed antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli [13]. At 100 μg mL−1 this nanocomposite killed 97.9% of bacteria after 120 min of exposure to visible light irradiation.

Cellular components such as membranes, proteins and DNA can interact with carbon based nanomaterials and initiate a sequence of nanomaterials/bacterial interactions that depend on colloidal energies and active bio-physicochemical interfaces [9]. Antibacterial effects of graphene derivatives can be caused by oxidative stress induced by membrane disruption [14]. The possible mechanisms behind antibacterial activity of GO is the damage of bacterial cell membranes as a result of reactive oxygen species generation and exceptionally sharp edges of GO [15,16]. Results of antimicrobial testing of graphene based nanomaterials are controversial [9]. A study of Hu et al. found a useful bactericidal effect of GO, supposedly because of bacterial membrane damage [17]. The results pointed out the “mechanical” membrane damage due to direct interaction of bacteria with graphene sheets. The study was focused on the physicochemical characteristics of graphene materials and their impact on their bactericide efficiency. The results were questioned by Ruiz et al. [18]. Barbolina et al. showed that antibacterial activity of GO strongly depends on GO purity: highly pure GO have no affect on the growth of Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, while the GO with residual S showed certain activity which masks the true effect of the material [19]. Also, significant antibacterial efficacy of nanohybrids based on GO and different metallic nanoparticles was observed: GO with silver nanoparticles [20,21], with ZnO nanoparticles [22], iron oxide [23], titanium oxides [24] etc. Apart from antibacterial, carbon nanomaterial can be using in imaging of both bacteria and fungi [25–28].

To consider any possible biological application, the influence of graphene based materials on live cells and organisms, and nature as well, should be tested. Results of toxicity studies on graphene related materials still give plenty room for further investigation of this topic [29].

Antibacterial effects of some monovalent cation salts of 12-phosphomolybdic acid (H3PMo12O40, MoPA) [30] and MoPA with vanadium were observed [31]. Mono-, di- and trivanadium-substituted MoPA showed antibacterial activity at concentration of 256 μg mL−1 and an inhibition rate above 70% [31]. He et al. studied antibacterial activity of M3PMo12O40·nH2O (M = NH4+, K+, Cs+) rhombic dodecahedral nanocrystals and showed that they have a strong inhibitory effect on the proliferation of Escherichia coli, when bacteria were exposed for 48 h to nanocrystals in concentration of 25 μg mL−1 [30]. But, the different bulk commercial materials such as (NH4)3[PMo12O40]·nH2O, K3[PMo12O40]·nH2O and Cs3[PMo12O40]·nH2O demonstrated poor antimicrobial activities under the same experiment conditions.

Considering previous reports which showed that insoluble salts of MoPA and MoPA with vanadium have antibacterial activity [30,31], we explore the possibility to improve this property of MoPA by modification with water dispersible GO. While the composites based on different forms of GO and polyoxometalates (POMs) were previously reported [32–36], their antibacterial properties were rarely studied [37]. In this paper we synthesized GO and nanocomposites based on GO with different amounts of MoPA. Earlier, we successfully produced electrochemically exfoliated graphene/molybdophosphoric acid nanohybrides with stable capacitance behavior [36]. At the same time, we tested the antibacterial potential of electrochemically exfoliated graphene sheets and established the very poor antibacterial activity of this type of graphene [38]. Considering the larger amount of functional groups in GO than in the structure of electrochemically exfoliated graphene, we expect higher modification of GO with MoPA. After the synthesis, we analyzed the structure and morphology of the obtained composites. Furthermore, GO, MoPA and nanocomposites based on GO and MoPA (GO-MoPA) were tested for antimicrobial activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide by modified Hummers method
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized by oxidation of graphite powder (Z-346, Timrex KS6 graphite, Bodio, Switzerland) using an improved Hummers method without NaNO3 in the reaction mixture [39,40]. In this procedure, graphite powder (1.0 g) was mixed with 23.3 mL of concentrated sulphuric acid (98%, Carl Roth) under constant stirring, on ice bath. After, KMnO4 (3 g, purchased from Merck, p. a.) was added slowly and the mixture was left in an ice bath for 15 min. Then, after adding 50 ml of MiliQ water in the reaction mixture, the temperature of the reaction mixture was increased to 40 °C for 30 min and to 95 °C for 15 min. At the end, the reaction mixture is poured into 170 ml of MiliQ water containing 5 ml of hydrogen peroxide (33%, Carl Roth). Then, the resulting solid was filtered with MiliQ water until the pH of the filtrate reached 7, and after was suspended in deionized (DI) water and additionally cleaned by dialysis against DI water to remove residual metal ions and acids. The solid graphene oxide (GO) was collected after vacuum drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide – MoPA nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (50 mg) was sonicated in 50 ml of methanol/water solution (1:1) for 30 min. Ethylene glycol (Alfa Aesar, p. a.) was added at concentration of 1% v/v. Then 500 mg of MoPA (Merck p. a.) was added (concentration of 5.2.10−3 M in solution), which resulted in a mass ratio of graphene oxide and MoPA of 1:10, and was stirred for 24 h, at room temperature. This sample was than cleaned from residual MoPA by dialysis (cut-off 3.5 kDa, Spectrum Labs) and dried under vacuum. The collected sample was named GO-MoPA-W.

The other sample was prepared by mixing 193 mg of GO, ethylene glycol (concentration of 1% v/v) and MoPA (concentration of 1.10−1 M) in 50 ml of methanol/water solution (1:1), thus obtaining the same 1:10 mass ratio of GO and MoPA. After 24 h of stirring at room temperature, the sample was first filtered over a membrane with pore size of 0.45 μm and then washed with 3 ml of methanol and dried in the air. This sample was named GO-MoPA-NW.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods: AFM, SEM EDS, FTIR and Raman measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) imaging was performed using a Quesant microscope. AFM was operated in tapping mode at room temperature, in air. As a probe Q-WM300 was used, for non-contact high frequency applications. Water dispersions of graphene oxide and GO modified with polyoxometalate were deposited on mica substrate by spin coating at a spin rate of 2500–3000 min−1 for 60 s and imaged after drying. For analysis of AFM images Gwyddion software was used [41]. For each sample, ten AFM images were recorded. AFM was also used for bacterial cell imaging. Bacterial cells were first fixed with 2.5% (vol/vol) glutaraldehyde solution (pH 6.8) for 2.5 h at 4 °C and washed in PBS. AFM measurements were performed as described for GO samples, and the different AFM parameters were determined for at least 30 bacterial cells per sample.

The morphology of the sample was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-6390LV) in vacuum at room temperature, with acceleration voltage of 15 kV. The elemental composition of samples was analyzed with energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS, Oxford Aztec X-max).

For UV-Vis measurements, the GO, GO-MoPA-W and GO-MoPA-NW dispersions (concentration of 0.125 mg ml−1) were prepared with MilliQ water and spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-Visible UV-2600 (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) spectrophotometer equipped with an integrated sphere (ISR-2600 Plus (for UV-2600)) in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm at 20 °C. The spectral resolution was 1 nm.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) imaging was performed on a JEOL JEM-1400 operated at 120 kV. Samples for TEM measurements were prepared by the sonication of GO, GO-MoPA-W and GO-MoPA-NW in MilliQ water except MoPA which was dissolved in methanol. The concentration of all dispersions was 2 mg mL−1. These dispersions were drop casted on lacey carbon film deposited on Cu grid with 200 Mesh, while MoPA was deposited also on Cu grid with graphene oxide support film.

For Raman spectroscopy measurements, we used a DXR Raman microscope (Thermo Scientific). As an excitation source, we applied 532 nm excitation line from a diode pumped, solid state laser with the power of 1 mW. The instrument was equipped with an Olympus optical microscope with infinity corrected confocal optics, 50 μm slit aperture, standard distance objective 50× and a CCD detector. The scattered light was analyzed by a spectrograph with a grating with 900 lines/mm. Acquisition time was 10 s with 10 scans repetitions. For spectra collection and manipulation, Thermo Scientific OMNIC software was used. Raman spectra were recorded at room temperature. For each sample, Raman spectra were recorded at 10 different locations on the surface of the sample.

Attenuated total reflectance (ATR) FTIR measurements were performed on a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) equipped with a single reflection diamond crystal. Typically, 64 scans were accumulated with resolution of 2 cm−1.

The antimicrobial activity of MoPA, synthesized samples of GO and GO-MoPA nanocomposites were investigated against seven different laboratory control strains of bacteria, i.e., the Gram-positive: Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 6538), Staphylococcus epidermidis (ATCC12228), Bacillus subtilis (ATCC6633); the Gram-negative: Escherichia coli (ATCC 10536), Klebsiella pneumoniae (ATCC 13883), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (ATCC 9027), and Salmonella abony (ATCC 6017). The tests for determination of minimum inhibitory concentration were performed in Muller Hinton broth.

Minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) was determined by the broth microdilution method according to Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute guidelines [42]. The tested compounds were suspended in distilled water, homogenized by vortexing and then diluted to the highest concentration. Twofold serial concentrations of the compounds were prepared in a 96-well microtiter plate (ranging from 31.2 to 1000 μg mL−1). The MIC determinations were performed in duplicate, and two positive growth controls were included. Each test was repeated three times. Tests were performed in the presence of triphenyltetrazolium chloride (TTC) as microbial cell growth indicator.

To provide better homogenization of samples, the second batch of samples was prepared by the adding of 0.1 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) to weighed sample prior to dilution with Muller Hinton broth to the highest concentration. For that purpose, agate mortar and pestle were used.

The morphological changes in GO upon interaction with MoPA were first studied by AFM. In Fig. 1 a, AFM image of synthesized GO is presented. The size of GO sheets varies from 230 to 1140 nm, while the average thickness of sheets is around 1.1 nm, which indicates that GO is in a form of single layer. As for GO with a large amount of MoPA (Fig. 1b), it can be observed that the morphology of GO has been significantly changed. GO sheets were covered with MoPA clusters so that GO lost its flatness. Also, GO sheets together with MoPA form large hybrid structures, with the height over 20 nm. The presence of MoPA clusters can be easily noticed at the height profile diagram (upper left corner of Fig. 1b), where MoPA presence can be observed as peaks in the profile. As for GO-MoPA-W sample, AFM image (Fig. 1c) shows dominantly graphene sheets similar to the non modified GO (Fig. 1a). On a flat surface of GO, clusters of MoPA can be observed as spherical structures. This indicates that during modification of graphene oxide with the low amount of MoPA, single graphene sheets retained their starting state: single layer.

Furthermore, we analyzed the surface roughness (Rq) of GO sheets before and after interaction with MoPA. The representative regions for Rq analysis are indicated by blue squares in Fig. 1 d, e and f. This parameter increased from 0.177 nm as measured for GO, to 0.256 nm for GO-MoPA-W sample (Fig. 1f). The multiple increases in Rq value is measured for GO-MoPA-NW sample (0.649 nm, Fig. 1e) which indicates the presence of large MoPA clusters on the surface of GO-MoPA which disrupts flat, graphene like morphology of GO.

The surface morphology of GO and GO-MoPA samples was characterized using SEM, and the representative micrographs are displayed in Fig. 2 . In the Fig. 2a and b, SEM micrographs of GO are presented. Both figures show the flat sheets with the length varying from few hundreds of nm up to a few microns. On the other hand, for GO-MoPA-NW sample, a particle like structure can be observed (Fig. 2c and d), which can be assigned to MoPA clusters on the surface of GO sheets. In the Fig. 2e and f, SEM micrographs at low and high magnification of GO-MoPA-W sample are presented. These micrographs show a combination of graphene sheets and agglomerated particles on the surface of GO-MoPA sample present in a much lower amount compared to GO-MoPA-NW. Therefore, SEM analysis has confirmed the results of AFM measurements, indicating the highest amount of MoPA in the sample of GO-MoPA-NW.

Chemical modification of GO was investigated by EDS spectroscopy. Table 1 shows the mass percentages of elements detected by SEM-EDS analysis of synthesized samples. As for GO, only C and O atoms were detected. In the sample of GO-MoPA-NW, apart from detection of C and O elements, P and Mo were also found. Both later elements stem from 12-molybdophosphoric acid. Detected Si stems from the support used for samples deposition but due to inhomogeneous deposition it was not detected in all samples. The sample of GO-MoPA-W contains C, O and Mo, where Mo indicates MoPA in nanohybrids present in a lower amount. These results suggest that MoPA is present in both prepared nanohybrids which was earlier confirmed by AFM and SEM imaging. Further analysis of the nature of interaction between GO and MoPA in both samples was investigated by Raman and FTIR spectroscopy.

The TEM image of GO is presented in Fig. 3 a. The sheet-like morphology can be observed as well as one and few-layered graphene oxide. The morphology of MoPA was also studied and it was noticed that acids created dark, large agglomerates. From TEM images of GO-MoPA-W (Fig. 3 c and d) and GO-MoPA-NW (Fig. 3 e and f) MoPA particles can be observed on the sheets of GO, which is the situation already observed in the AFM and SEM images. Only a few MoPA clusters are noticed as dark particles in the sample of GO-MoPA-W. On the contrary, TEM images of GO-MoPA-NW show well-distributed particles distributed over the sheet-like structure. Larger MoPA aggregates were observed in the GO-MoPA-W sample. Kim et al. suggested that larger particles were obtained from aggregation of POMs through strong interactions with oxygen groups on the graphene sheet [33]. Thus, the observed large MoPA particles in GO-MoPA-W are the result of a strong interaction between GO and MoPA. The nature of this interaction will be discussed in further text.

The optical properties of GO-MoPA composites were investigated by UV Vis spectroscopy. In Fig. 4 , UV Vis spectra of GO, GO-MoPA-W and GO-MoPA-NW are displayed. In the UV Vis absorbance spectrum of GO a broad band at around 240 nm is present and stems from π-π* transitions in the sp2 domains (Fig. 4, black curve) while the additional shoulder band at 308 nm is assigned to n-π* transition of CO bonds. In the spectra of GO-MoPA-W, the band which stems from π-π* transitions is a bit shifted (at 245 nm) and lower intensity, while the band at 305 nm can be also observed. These small shifts in the band positions can implicate the establishing of the interaction between GO and MoPA.

On the contrary, in the UV-Vis spectrum of GO-MoPA-NW (Fig. 4, red lower curve), bands which are characteristic for GO cannot be observed. This is probably due to the covering of GO surface with large amount of MoPA, as it was observed earlier by microscopic techniques.
The nature of interaction will be investigated in the following text.

The molecular structure of GO-MoPA samples was analyzed by FTIR spectroscopy, a very appropriate method for consideration of characteristic vibration bands of the metal-oxides in POMs as well as vibrations of different functional groups in GO. The ATR-FTIR spectra of GO, MoPA and GO-MoPA-NW and GO-MoPA-W nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 5 . The graphene oxide is characterized by the presence of oxygen containing functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl and epoxy groups, randomly distributed in the sample. As these functional groups may interact with each other and thus cause shift in characteristic vibration frequencies, some disagreements in literature in assignment of the modes that involve adjacent configurations can be observed. Acik et al. combined infrared absorption measurements with theoretically simulated infrared frequencies and intensities using density functional theory (DFT) calculations [43]. Interpretation of our FTIR spectra are based on vibration modes assignments by these authors as well as literature survey given by Tucureanu et al. [44].

The band located at about 1720 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibration of carboxyl groups or conjugated carbonyl groups of GO. Its position is the same in GO and in both nanocomposites. In-plane vibrations of the skeletal CC band of the hexagonal aromatic ring from the graphene sheet or corresponding to the remaining sp2 character from the non oxide graphitic network, is overlapped with the hydroxyl band. This band is observed in both nanocomposites, but it is red shifted from 1614 cm−1 in GO to 1580 cm−1 in GO-MoPA-W nanocomposite with low content of MoPA. The band observed in GO at 1390 cm−1 is assigned to an O-H group; this group in GO-MoPA-W is blue shifted to 1433 cm−1. Bands at 1230 and 1040 cm−1 stem from C-O-C groups in GO. Some shifts and changes in intensity of bands of oxygen-containing groups in nanocomposites in comparison to these bands in GO indicate interaction between GO and MoPA. ATR-FTIR spectra of GO-MoPA nanocomposites are dominated by the strong bands coming from MoPA. This acid has four characteristic bands in IR spectra at 1057 cm−1 (νas of P-Oa), 959 cm−1 (νas of Mo = Od), 890 cm−1 (νas of Mo-Ob-Mo) and 754 cm−1 (νas of Mo-Oc- Mo). From the presented spectra, it can be concluded that bands of the Keggin anion of MoPA are presented in spectra of nanocomposites, thus demonstrating retained structure of Keggin anion in both samples. Bands at 1040 and 970 cm−1 originating from epoxy groups in GO are overlapped with the strong stretching P-O and Mo=Od bands from MoPA, causing appearance of a shoulder of band at 1057 cm−1 and broadening and slight red shift of the band at 959 cm−1. ATR-FTIR spectra also indicate interaction of Keggin anion and GO, as bands assigned to asymmetric stretching Mo = Od and Mo-Ob-Mo vibrations are slightly red shifted. However, asymmetric Mo-Oc-Mo vibration is blue shifted, especially in the GO-MoPA-W sample, where this band is shifted for more than 20 cm−1, while in GO-MoPA-NW, the shift is only a few cm−1. Rocchiccioli-Deltcheff et al. have systematically considered the influence of cation-size on the vibration spectra of α and β isomers of Keggin type anion with different central X atom. This influence is the most obvious on the M-Od (M is the addenda atom, typically Mo or W) stretching frequencies which decreases as the cation size increases. The effect is explained by weakening of anion-anion interactions of the electrostatic type with increasing of cation size. The mentioned influence is more complex for other M-O vibrations such as (M-Ob-M) (“inter” bridge stretchings) and (M-Oc-M) (“intra” bridge stretchings) which have also some bending character [45]. This can explain different shift of Mo-Ob-Mo and Mo-Oc-Mo bands observed in ATR-FTIR of our nanocomposites. Differences in spectra of GO-MoPA-W and GO-MoPA-NW may be explained by the excess of MoPA in samples washed only with a small amount of methanol. In sample GO-MoPA-W, the excess of acid is removed by dialysis.

The data obtained from FTIR spectra are not in accordance with the reference data where some authors have found no change in the position of characteristic bands of MoPA modified with GO or activated carbon [46], while others observed blue shift in the position of Mo-Oc-Mo vibration and red shift of P-O, Mo=Od and Mo-Ob-Mo bands when rGO was modified with MoPA [33]. When MoPA ions interacted with oxygen and alkyl functionalized graphene, Tessonnier et al. observed shift of the Mo-Oc-Mo vibration up to 70 cm−1 and linear decrease of this band wavenumber with acid loadings [47]. The differences in FTIR spectra of different POMs (of Keggin type) supported on carbon nanomaterials may be explained by different synthesis methods and different functionalizations of the graphene surface which essentially determine the interaction of POMs and graphene.

Therefore all the changes observed in FTIR spectra of our nanocomposites may be explained by non-covalent interaction of MoPA with GO. Shih et al. [48] have shown by a comparative experimental and molecular dynamics simulation study that colloidal stability and surface activity of GO aqueous solution is pH dependent. At low pH, the carboxyl groups at the edges of GO sheets are protonated such that the GO sheets become less hydrophilic and can form aggregates. Our nanocomposites are synthesized from very acid solution (pH = 1) where carboxyl groups of GO are protonated. The interaction of GO and MoPA is realized by hydrogen bonding between positively charged groups at the edges of GO sheets and Keggin anion of MoPA. Our results have shown that under the applied experimental conditions the structure of Keggin anion is preserved. However, the interactions between GO and MoPA cause some change in the symmetry of the Keggin anion anchored to the surface of GO sheets, resulting in the obtained FTIR spectra.

Raman spectroscopy is a widely used tool for the investigation of structural properties of carbon based nanomaterials, particularly those which contain conjugated and double C-C bonds [49]. These types of bonds lead to high intensity bands in the Raman spectrum. In a Raman spectrum of graphene oxide (Fig. 6 , curve 1), two prominent bands can be noticed. First one is so-called G band which stems from in-plane stretching vibrations of sp2 bonds and it is usually located at 1580 cm −1. The next one is a D band, also known as a disorder band. This feature is the consequence of defects in the graphene like structure, the edges and broken symmetry by sp2 dangling bonds. In the case of graphene oxide, a low intensity band at 2660 cm−1 can also be observed. This one is called 2D or G’ band and it is a second-order overtone of a different in-plane vibration [50].

The bands in Raman spectra of GO, GO-MoPA-W and GO-MoPA-NW were quantitatively analyzed in terms of: positions of D, G and 2D bands (denoted as ωD, ωG and ω2D), intensity ratio between D and G bands (ID/IG) and ratio between ω2D/ωG. These results are presented in Table 2 and they reflect changes of the carbon honeycomb structure due to the interaction with the various amounts of MoPA.

The D band was located at the almost same position when different amounts of MoPA were supported, which was expected considering that this band is dispersive and thus its position is dependent on the laser excitation energy and not on the dopant amount [50].

The largest upshift was observed for the GO-MoPA-NW sample, where the position of the G band shifted from 1592 to 1605 cm−1. In the case of GO-MoPA-W sample, the G band again shifted to higher wavelengths, for just 2 cm−1. The microscopic stress or strain or changes in the layer number, p- or n-doping, can cause the change in the G band position [51]. The shift as a result of stress/strain is reported to be much higher (above 20 cm−1), whereas in the presented samples it varies from 2 to 13 cm−1. Also the changes in the number of layers is less probable considering that GO is stabilized with polar functional groups which cannot be reduced under used conditions. However, the functionalization of graphene sheets can cause changes in the electron density in the graphene structure. Therefore, the shift in G band position observed in Raman spectra of our nanocomposites may be a consequence of electron density changes in graphene structure due to interaction of MoPA with GO sheets. The large upshift in the G band position can be explained by charge transfer between graphene sheets and MoPA [33]. Therefore, MoPA ions withdraw electrons from graphene sheets, causing the lowering of the electron density in GO. This effect is more distinct in the GO-MoPA-NW than for GO-MoPA-W which is a result of lower amount of MoPA in the latter sample. This is in accordance with an ab initio study on the structural and electronic properties of phosphotungstic acid (H3PW12O40, WPA) on the graphene (G) layer which has shown the possibility of formation of the G-WPA complex from energetic perspective through electron transfer between G and WPA [52].

The shift in 2D band was also noticed. Compared to GO, for both samples with MoPA, 2D band was upshifted significantly. The position of this band can be shifted due to decrease of the layers number or doping: blue-shifts on hole doping and red shifts on doping with electrons [53].
Considering that both G and 2D bands were blue shifted for GO samples with MoPA, we can conclude that MoPA caused p-doping of GO.

The integrated intensity ratio of the D and G band (ID/IG) indicate the size of sp2 domain in graphene based materials. For GO, ID/IG is 1.117 while for GO-MoPA-NW and GO-MoPA-W samples this parameter is higher (1.369 and 1.315, respectively). These calculations imply that with dispersion of MoPA over the GO surface, the structural disorder has been increased and thus the size of sp2 domains is lowered.

All our results have confirmed existence of the electrostatic interaction of MoPA with GO sheets. From one side, this interaction is realized by hydrogen bonding between positively charged groups at the edges of GO sheets and the Keggin anion of MoPA, causing some changes in its geometry. However, Raman spectroscopy results have shown that charge transfer between MoPA and GO sheets is also realized.

The literature survey on this topic shows similar conclusions but mainly for interaction of GO and POMs of Keggin type. Kim et al. explained interaction between rGO and MoPA through the electrostatic interaction between the Keggin anion and the protonated surface functional groups of rGO, acting as a counterion [33]. Based on UV-Vis spectra and observed shift in the band gap of the MoPA-rGO nanocomposite compared to MoPA, the same authors concluded that energy levels of MoPA are changed due to electron transfer from MoPA to rGO. Tessonnier et al. found that shift in the position of M-Oc-M band in rGO-MoPA nanocomposite may be explained by partial delocalization of H+ between the Keggin anion and rGO sheet thus enabling electrostatic interaction between the Keggin anion and protonated GO sheet [47]. The separation of the molecularly dispersed MoPA on the surfaces of alkyl functionalized rGO was also taken in consideration [47]. Li et al. have concluded that electron transfer from photo-reduced POMs to GO leads to reduction of GO during UV irradiation of aqueous solution of POM, GO and isopropanol [32].

As suspensions of GO and functionalized GO samples are very dark coloured, especially in higher concentrations, it was necessary to use triphenyltetrazolium chloride (TTC) as microbial cell growth indicator. TTC is a non-coloured substance which becomes red after reduction in microbial cell. The results of determined minimal inhibitor concentration for MoPA, GO and two samples of GO functionalized with MoPA are presented in Table 3 .

According to the experimental procedure, it would be expected that the dialysis step of sample GO-MoPA-W could lead to more efficient removal of MoPA excess in comparison to the GO-MoPA-NW sample. Some effect of GO-MoPA-W against Bacillus subtilis could be attributed to the remaining structure of GO layers, enabling the direct contact of bacterial cell membranes and GO. Bacillus subtilis is a facultative anaerobe bacteria, most commonly found in soil and plants and usually ascribed the beneficial effects of Bacillus subtilis spores on the balance on the gastrointestinal microflora of humans. Although the pathogenic potential of Bacillus subtilis is commonly pronounced as low, Bacillus subtilis can cause some opportunistic infections, such as sepsis in immunocompromised patients and conjunctivitis in people who already have chronic lesions or irritations of the conjunctiva. In some cases, Bacillus subtilis strains exhibited resistance to penicillin, erythromycin, rifampin, novobiocin, and chloramphenicol [54].
On the other hand, Bacillus subtilis has been used for long time as a biological control agent against different plant bacterial diseases [55].
From that point of view, it is important to know the potential destructive effect of GO and it functionalized samples to the balance of soil ecosystems in case of their discharge into environment.

Neither of the GO-MoPA-NW samples exhibit antibacterial effect, nor MoPA itself, and it could be supposed either that a different kind of connection between MoPA and GO occurred, or the superfluous MoPA remains.

In order to investigate the synergistic effects of antibacterial agents, we use the obtained values of MIC and calculated the fractional inhibitory concentration (FIC) of both MoPA and GO. The value of FIC index for Bacillus subtilis is 5 while for other studied strains these values are 2. These results indicate that the antibacterial agents GO and MoPA have indifference antibacterial activity towards Staphylococcus aureus, Staphylococcus epidermidis, Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Salmonella abony. The value of FIC indicate the antagonistic nature of the interaction between GO and MoPA in the complex GO-MoPA-W towards Bacillus subtilis.

Those preliminary results of antimicrobial activity testing encouraged us to try to achieve better homogenization of tested suspensions by adding DMSO. DMSO is a solvent often used for similar purposes. In contrast to above, in the repeated experiment with prior homogenization step with 0.1 mL of DMSO, all determined MIC were >1000 μg/mL. In a control test, the added volume of DMSO does not exhibit antimicrobial effects to the chosen bacteria and yeast. Thus, if we take into consideration the supposed mechanism of antibacterial ability of GO to cause the physical damages on bacterial cell membranes by sharp edges [17], the obtained results could be better understood. Therefore, the homogenization of GO, GO-MoPA-W and GO-MoPA-NW with DMSO in mortar with pestle could destroy the structure, mild edges, and so DMSO as “protection layer” is unable to directly affect to bacterial cell membrane.

For visualization of Bacillus subtilis before and after treatment with GO-MoPA composites, AFM was used. Selected results are presented in Fig. 7 . The control, untreated Bacillus subtilis (Fig. 7a), had typically granular, rod-shaped cells. The treatment with composite GO-MoPA-NW did not induce significant changes in bacterial cells morphology (Fig. 7b), displaying similar structure as the control. The large changes in morphology are observed after treatment with GO-MoPA-W in concentration of 250 μg mL−1 (Fig. 7c). Significant reduction in bacterial height from around 180 nm as measured for control to 75 nm may suggest the loss of membrane integrity leading to leakage of intracellular compounds and consequently lowering the height of bacterial cells. The root mean square (RMS) roughness values are also measured in AFM images: 63.2 nm for control, 59.6 nm for bacteria treated with GO-MoPA-NW and 67.2 nm for GO-MoPA-W. Our study showed that the increase in surface roughness indicated the cell membrane damage [56]. Thus AFM analysis further confirm the antibacterial effects of GO-MoPA-W on Bacillus subtilis.

Importantly, there are many aspects of GO structure that can affect its antibacterial behavior. Antibacterial activity of GO as well as other graphene based nanomaterials depends on edges sharpness, sheet size, surface area, roughness, hydrophilicity, dispersibility and functionalization [9]. These properties can individually or interactively influence the contacts and effects of GO towards bacteria. We observed the following morphological and structural properties of GO and nanocomposites and their effects on bacteria:
•
As can be observed in AFM images of GO, GO-MoPA-NW and GO-MoPA-W, all samples have very sharp edges. Gurunathan et al. showed that the concentration of extracellular RNA was high when the bacterial cells were exposed to GO or rGO compared to the control sample [57]. The results were due to destruction of the bacterial cell membrane with the sharp edges of the nanosheets and the higher membrane damage was observed in the Gram-positive bacteria, deficient in the external membrane, than in the Gram-negative ones (with the external membrane). Our sample showed only poor activity toward one of Gram-positive bacteria (Bacillus subtilis) which suggest that in our case the sharp edges are not a dominant bactericidal factor.
•
It was noticed that the size of the graphene sheet has an important effect on antibacterial activity: small sheets can rupture the bacterial membrane, actually their phospholipid layer, whereas the large sheets can inhibit the bacterial growth by a cell wrapping mechanism [58]. In our sample of GO, small sheets are dominantly present (with surface mostly below 0.210 μm2). Therefore it would be expected a good antibacterial activity. But our results showed that the small size of GO sheets did not have an effect on antibacterial activity.
•
The surface roughness has also an impact on antibacterial behavior of graphene sheets. Increased surface roughness improves bacterial cell adhesion and some functionalities showed antibacterial activity due to changes in a membrane permeability, which can destroy bacterial cells. Such surfaces have extensive roughness or ridges with the high incidence which can in contact with bacterial cells cause cracking of walls [59]. Upon modification with MoPA, surface roughness of GO was increased dramatically, from 0.177 nm to 0.256 nm for the GO-MoPA-W sample and up to 0.649 nm for the GO-MoPA-NW sample. Expected increased antibacterial activity with the increased surface roughness was not observed probably because the roughness stems from absorbed MoPA which did not show any antibacterial effects.
•
Residual S in GO structure remaining after synthesis can induce a false positive antibacterial effect if [19] S is present in 1.6% or higher, while at lower S content GO do not show antibacterial effect. In our samples, S was not detected which indicated that GO was perfectly cleaned and that the studied antibacterial effects stems exclusively from GO and obtained nanocomposites.

Considering we followed the structural and morphological aspects of synthesized composites and showed they did not exhibit antibacterial effects, we are assuming that observed mild antibacterial behavior of GO and GO-MoPA-W is related to photochemical reactions: production of reactive oxygen species in the presence of oxygen. But further experiments will be conducted in the future in order to understand the mechanism of antibacterial effects of both GO and GO-MoPA-W.

The challenge in further plans for testing such materials to antibacterial activity and their possible application for that purpose has to be connected to investigation of other POMs of Keggin type and the preparation of samples of smaller particle sized powder as possible. The obtained results also allow more detailed future investigation of detected antibacterial effects of GO and tested samples functionalized with MoPA, including the mechanism of the confirmed antibacterial activity and its possible bio application. Special attention has to be paid to the toxicity of the tested GO based materials to the environment.

In this study we prepared nanocomposites based on GO and MoPA and analyzed their morphology, structural properties and antibacterial activity. We showed that interactions between GO and MoPA are realized which cause changes in the Keggin anion symmetry, the increase in structural disorder and the lowering of electron density in the GO structure. Even though literature survey showed that both GO and MoPA have antibacterial activity, we have observed the only very poor antibacterial behavior of GO (MIC was 250 μg/mL) and nanocomposite GO-MoPA-W against Bacillus subtilis. We analyzed different morphological and structural effects of GO and obtained composites on antibacterial activity such as edges sharpness, the sheet size, surface roughness and purity of materials. But, neither prepared nanocomposites nor GO showed any significant activity towards applied bacteria. Only a mild effect of GO-MoPA-W against Bacillus subtilis is noticed, but again it can be ascribed to GO.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Research was supported by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia, project no. 172003 and 172043.
